coredata.dc:title: Sulfuric acid induced-synthesis coupled with ethanol extraction-water precipitation purification method for orange fluorescent carbon dots with dual-emission: Application for methyl blue detection and cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Lijuan
Wang, Dandan
Gong, Yingyu
Quan, Tian
Tao, Yongqing
Liu, Shaochi
Wang, Luchun
Wang, Junji
Tian, Meng
Gao, Die
coredata.dc:description: In this work, by adjusting the sulfuric acid content in reaction solvent of ethanol, orange fluorescent carbon dots (O-FCDs) with dual-emission wavelength and blue fluorescent carbon dots (B-FCDs) with single-emission wavelength were successfully prepared using 1,3-dihydroxynaphthalene as precursor. Coupling with ethanol extraction-water precipitation purification method, pure O-FCDs and B-FCDs with yields of 9.0 % and 21.3 %, quantum yields (QYs) of 43.0 % and 13.7 % were obtained, respectively. The structures and optical properties of O-FCDs and B-FCDs were investigated by TEM, AFM, Raman, FT-IR, XPS, UV–vis, fluorescence analysis etc. The results revealed that sulfuric acid promoted the carbonization and the oxidation of precursor in the reaction process. In comparison with the B-FCDs, O-FCDs showed narrower lattice spacing and band gap, demonstrating the important role of sulfur-doping in fluorescence tuning. Additionally, O-FCDs showed good sensitivity for methyl blue with a linear response range of 0.05–100 μM (LOD was 20 nM) and the satisfactory results were obtained when O-FCDs were applied to the detection of methyl blue in real fish sample. Moreover, two FCDs showed good biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity proved by MTT experiment, while, O-FCDs showed better cell imaging effects than that of B-FCDs. Therefore, the O-FCDs had a broad application prospect as sensing platform in detection of methyl blue and for imaging in biological field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfuric acid
Orange fluorescent carbon dots
Dual-emission wavelength
Blue fluorescent carbon dots
Methyl blue
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation processes of O-FCDs and B-FCDs
Sensitivity detection of methyl blue by using O-FCDs
Determination of methyl blue in actual sample
Application of FCDs in living cells
Preparation and characterization of two FCDs
Characterization of the structural and morphological properties of FCDs
Study of the optical properties of FCDs
Fluorescence sensing of O-FCDs for methyl blue
Fluorescent response to methyl blue by O-FCDs
Investigation the quenching mechanism of O-FCDs - methyl blue system

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by a joint program of Luzhou Government and Southwest Medical University (2020LZXNYDJ25) and a School-level project of Southwest Medical University (2021ZKMS040).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, c) TEM images and corresponding lateral size distributions of B-FCDs (a) and O-FCDs (c). (b,d) HR-TEM images of B-FCDs (b) and O-FCDs (d). e-f. AFM images and corresponding height profiles of B-FCDs (e) and O-FCDs (f). g-h. Raman spectra of the B-FCDs (g) and O-FCDs (h).

a. FT-IR spectra of B-FCDs and O-FCDs. b. XPS spectra of B-FCDs. c. XPS spectra of O-FCDs. d-e. C 1s spectrum of the B-FCDs (d) and O-FCDs (e). f-g. O 1s spectrum of the B-FCDs (f) and O-FCDs (g). h. S2p spectrum of the O-FCDs.

a. UV–vis absorption spectra of methyl blue, B-FCDs and O-FCDs. b-c. Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of O-FCDs (b) and B-FCDs (c) (Insets showed the FCDs solution under visible light and UV light of 365 nm). d-e. Fluorescence emission spectra of O-FCDs (d) and B-FCDs (e) at different excitation wavelengths. f-g. Bandgap energies of B-FCDs (f) and G-FCDs (g). h. The sulfuric acid regulation luminescence mechanism.

a. Fluorescence emission spectra of O-FCDs in the presence of methyl blue with the concentration ranges of 0–100 μM. b. Local fluorescence spectrum emission spectra of O-FCDs in the presence of methyl blue with the concentration ranges of 0–100 μM. c-d. The relationship curves between S1/S2 and concentrations of 0.05–100 μM (c) and 0.05–1.0 μM (d).

a-b. Fluorescence decay curves of O-FCDs with or without methyl blue at emission wavelengths of 535 nm (a) and 575 nm (b). c. The overlap between fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of the O-FCDs and the UV absorption spectrum of methyl blue. d-e. Stern-Volmer values of methyl blue at the emission wavelengths of 535 nm (d) and 575 nm (e) at the temperatures of 20 °C, 30 °C and40°C.
a. Effect of B-FCDs on the proliferation of cholangiocarcinoma cells. b. Effect of O-FCDs on the proliferation of cholangiocarcinoma cells.

a. The fluorescence images of cell imaging under different concentrations (25–500 μg·mL-1) of O-FCDs and B-FCDs. b. The fluorescent images of cells treated with 500 μg·mL-1 of O-FCDs and B-FCDs at different times (30 min–6 h). c. Image of cells in bright field and the fluorescence image of cells incubated with O-FCDs at optimal conditions.
Preparation, ethanol extraction-water precipitation and purification of O-FCDs and B-FCDs.
Elemental analysis of B-FCDs and O-FCDs.
Analysis of methyl blue in real fish by using the O-FCDs based sensor (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Sulfuric acid induced-synthesis coupled with ethanol extraction-water precipitation purification method for orange fluorescent carbon dots with dual-emission: Application for methyl blue detection and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: L.J. Yang and D.D. Wang contributed equally to this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, by adjusting the sulfuric acid content in reaction solvent of ethanol, orange fluorescent carbon dots (O-FCDs) with dual-emission wavelength and blue fluorescent carbon dots (B-FCDs) with single-emission wavelength were successfully prepared using 1,3-dihydroxynaphthalene as precursor. Coupling with ethanol extraction-water precipitation purification method, pure O-FCDs and B-FCDs with yields of 9.0 % and 21.3 %, quantum yields (QYs) of 43.0 % and 13.7 % were obtained, respectively. The structures and optical properties of O-FCDs and B-FCDs were investigated by TEM, AFM, Raman, FT-IR, XPS, UV–vis, fluorescence analysis etc. The results revealed that sulfuric acid promoted the carbonization and the oxidation of precursor in the reaction process. In comparison with the B-FCDs, O-FCDs showed narrower lattice spacing and band gap, demonstrating the important role of sulfur-doping in fluorescence tuning. Additionally, O-FCDs showed good sensitivity for methyl blue with a linear response range of 0.05–100 μM (LOD was 20 nM) and the satisfactory results were obtained when O-FCDs were applied to the detection of methyl blue in real fish sample. Moreover, two FCDs showed good biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity proved by MTT experiment, while, O-FCDs showed better cell imaging effects than that of B-FCDs. Therefore, the O-FCDs had a broad application prospect as sensing platform in detection of methyl blue and for imaging in biological field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), one kinds of the most promising luminescent nanomaterials, have been incessantly garnering enormous attention resulting from their various advantages such as good photobleaching resistance, easy to modify, low cost, good biocompatibility etc [1]. Because of these outstanding advantages, CDs have been widely used in sensing, catalysis, drug loading, anti-counterfeiting, LED device manufacturing and other fields [2–5]. It can be obviously seen that for different applications, CDs with different luminescent colours need to be developed to meet the needs. However, compared with low-wavelength emission CDs (blue to green ones), long-wavelength emission CDs such as yellow, orange and red ones have better application values, and hence they are preferable for biological and medical applications. For examples, CDs with yellow, orange and red fluorescence are often needed to achieve the preparation of multi colour LED lights [6–8]. The CDs from orange to red fluorescence have great potential in biological imaging because of the advantages of low interference and high tissue penetration [9,10]. In the field of sensing, CDs of all colours have good applications, especially long-wavelength emission CDs such as yellow to red, which also have better application potential than blue or green fluorescent CDs in detection sensitivity due to the low interference [11].

Till now, the preparation of long-wavelength emission CDs is still a challenge, and there is no relatively unified synthesis method, mainly because the formation process and fluorescence mechanism of long-wavelength emission CDs have not been clearly explained. As reported, methods such as heteroatom doping and acid-mediated synthesis strategy have been employed as effective ways to adjust the fluorescent properties of CDs [12,13]. Particularly, heteroatom doping has been reported to be one of the most efficient methods. More specifically, on one hand, doping can not only control the surface state of the CDs, change the structure of the framework of CDs, lead to defects in the CDs framework, resulting in CDs band gap contraction and fluorescence redshift [14,15]. On the other hand, doping can also tune the composition, electronic density, electron transfer and other intrinsic properties of CDs, resulting in enhancing the photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) [16]. Recently, various long-wavelength emission CDs have been fabricated through heteroatom doping method. For instance, Song and co-workers have successfully used 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid and 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid as precursors to prepare B, N and S co-doped orange fluorescence CDs [17]. Shamsipur and et al have manufactured N and S co-doped CDs which can separately emit blue, red, and yellow fluorescence under acidic, neutral and alkaline conditions through hydrothermal treatment of glucose and 3-nitroaniline in aqueous sulfuric acid solution [18]. N and P co-doped red CDs have been developed by Sun’s group by using o-phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid as the raw materials using a facile hydrothermal method [19]. It can be seen from most studies that the preparation of long-wavelength emission CDs is mostly through doping of multiple heteroatoms. However, the reasons leading to the fluorescence colour change are multiple, and it is not clear whether the fluorescence colour change is caused by the common doping of multiple heteroatoms or by a single heteroatom doping. At present, a few studies are gradually exploring the mechanism of fluorescence colour change which caused by single doping, double doping or multi-doping. However, most of studies are based on the comparison between single-doped and double-doped or multi-doped [19,20], and there is almost no study on the fluorescence color changes of single-doped and undoped heteroatomic CDs. Therefore, in order to explain the effect of doping on fluorescence colour change more clearly, it is meaningful to explore the influence of single heteroatom doping and without doping on the change of fluorescence color of CDs.

In addition, it is well-known that is the most widely used field for CDs. In this field, the sensitivity and anti-interference ability of fluorescent probes are very important for the detection of target analytes. Ratiometric fluorescent probe contains dual-emission bands which exhibits higher sensitivity than single-emission. Fluorescence of ratio fluorescence analysis is less disturbed by ambient noise and other components. To date, most dual-emission fluorescent probes reported are composed of organic dyes, quantum dots or carbon nanomaterials [21,22]. The synthesis methods of these probes are relatively complex, and most of them are toxic and have poor biocompatible. Fluorescent CDs are very suitable for the preparation of ratio fluorescent probes due to their simple preparation method, adjustable fluorescence emission wavelength, good biocompatibility and low toxicity. Nevertheless, it is worth noting that most reported CDs produce only one emission band under a single excitation, which limits their application in the field of highly sensitive sensing [23,24]. Thus, herein, the preparation of single heteroatom doped long-wavelength emission CDs with ratio fluorescence characteristics shows important application value.

Besides, in order to improve the sensitivity of sensing, in addition to anti-interference ability, the QY of CDs is also an important factor. The purification of CDs is the main factor affecting the QY. In the purification process, some impurities and fluorescent small molecules with fluorescence characteristics can be removed. The commonly used purification measures are dialysis and silica column chromatography. Although dialysis can be used effectively for the purification of CDs, the dialysis process is often affected by solvents. For example, acid in the solvent will affect the properties of the dialysis membrane, and even destroy it, resulting in dialysis failure. For silica column chromatography, the purification process is complex, which also tends to lead to the low yield of CDs. Moreover, the separation process requires a mass of chemical solvents, which is not environmentally friendly. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a purification method for CDs with simple steps, high purification efficiency and high yield. Ethanol extraction-water precipitation purification method is a commonly used method for extracting effective parts of traditional Chinese medicines because it has the advantages of safe and green, simple operation, high efficiency and high purification efficiency. The principle is to take advantage of the polarity difference between the target substance and other substances in water and ethanol. Considering the polarity difference between CDs and other unreacted substances and impurities, this method may be used to purify CDs.

Herein, by using 1,3-dihydroxynaphthalene, which only contains C, H, O elements, as precursor, sulfuric acid (S doping)-ethanol solution or ethanol as solvent, orange fluorescence CDs (O-FCDs) with dual-emission wavelength and blue fluorescence CDs (B-FCDs) with single-emission wavelength were synthesized. The ethanol extraction-water precipitation purification method was successfully used to purify-two kinds of FCDs and the fluorescent FCDs powder with high QYs and high yields were obtained. The structure and optical properties of two FCDs were evaluated by TEM, Raman, AFM, FT-IR, UV–vis and fluorescence analysis. By comparing the structure, functional groups and optical properties of O-FCDs and B-FCDs, the influence of the sulfur-doping on the tuning of fluorescence colour of CDs was predicted. In addition, due to the abundant groups such as carbonyl carboxyl and hydroxyl, O-FCDs can be used for methyl blue sensing. The sensitivity and stability of the sensor and the fluorescence quenching mechanism of O-FCDs by methyl blue were systematically studied. Meanwhile, the practical application of O-FCDs sensor platform in fish sample was systematically studied. Finally, the toxicity and biocompatibility of two FCDs were explored and cell imaging were done on cholangiocarcinoma cells.

1,3-Dihydroxynaphthalene (Purity > 99 %) was bought from Bidepharm (Shanghai, China). Quinine sulfate (>99 %) was purchased from Acros Co. ltd. Methyl blue (>98 %), methylene blue (>98 %), malachite green (>98 %), and hematoxylin (>95 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd. Rhodamine 6G (>99 %) was obtained from Adamas Reagent Co., ltd. 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) and penicillin-streptomycin and (>98 %) were supplied from Beyotime Biotechnology. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) and trypsin were obtained from Hyclone. All reagents for ions were obtained from Chengdu Kelong Co., Ltd.

UV–vis properties of O-FCDs, B-FCDs and methyl blue were gained through UV-2600 spectrometer (Shimadzu). The fluorescence analysis of O-FCDs and B-FCDs were investigated on a fluorescence spectrometer (LS-55, Perkin Elmer). The functional group characteristics and elemental compositions of O-FCDs and B-FCDs were obtained by using a FT-IR spectrophotometer (IR Affinity-1S, Shimadzu) and an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Electron). The morphology characterizations of FCDs were explored on a transmission electron microscope (JEOL-2100F, JEOL). Raman spectra were acquired by using a Horiba Scientific LabRAM HR Evolution Raman spectrometer. AFM images were characterized using Bruker Dimension Icon. The fluorescence lifetimes were calculated using a lifetime and steady state spectrometer (FLS980, Perkin Elmer). MTT assay was completed on a microplate reader (synergy H1, Biotek).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation processes of O-FCDs and B-FCDs
ce:section.ce:para: O-FCDs and B-FCDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. In details, 0.5 g of 1,3-dihydroxynaphthalene was added into 30 mL of sulfuric acid-ethanol solution (sulfuric acid: 2 mL or 0 mL) and ultrasound for 5 min. Then, transferring the mixture into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (50 mL). Thereafter, the products obtained under heating at 220 °C for 2 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the FCDs solution was further gained by filtering through a microfiltration membrane (0.22 μm). The ethanol extraction-water precipitation purification method was mainly carried out through three steps. Firstly, triploid deionized water (90 mL) was added to (30 mL) form FCDs precipitate and the FCDs precipitate was obtained after centrifugation at 7500 g for 10 min. Secondly, the precipitate was dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol to obtain the ethanol solution of FCDs. Thirdly, the solution was dialysis in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h (secondary purification). Finally, the obtained FCDs were dried under vacuum for further use. The QYs of FCDs were calculated by using the formula shown in Supporting Materials . The Scheme 1 showed the preparation processes of FCDs.

The stabilities of O-FCDs were studied as followed: For pH stability: O-FCDs (1 mg·L-1) were dissolved in various pH (0–14) water and the fluorescence intensities were recorded under the emission wavelength of 535 and 575 nm by a excitation wavelength of 490 nm. For metal ion stability: various ions (including Ba2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Ge3+, Er3+, Mg2+, K+, Ni2+, Na+ and Au+) solution (0.3 mL, 1 mM) was added into the O-FCDs solution (2.7 mL, 1 mg·L-1).The fluorescence intensities of O-FCDs after adding various ions solution at the same experimental situation were recorded. To explore the time stability of O-FCDs, the fluorescence intensity changes of O-FCDs solution were detected at different time point (0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 and 10 min).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity detection of methyl blue by using O-FCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the sensitivity of O-FCDs for methyl blue, different concentrations of methyl blue with a range of 0–100 μM (300 μL) were added in the O-FCDs ethanol solution (2.7 mL). The fluorescence intensities of solutions before and after adding methyl blue were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 490 nm and emission wavelength of 535 nm and 575 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of methyl blue in actual sample
ce:section.ce:para: Cutting the fish into small pieces and weighing out 50 g. Next, adding deionized water (50 mL) into the pieces. Using a homogenizer to pound small pieces of fish to homogenate, and 500 mL of deionized water was added to dilute the homogenate. Finally, the fish sample was gained from filtering through a microfiltration membrane (0.45 μm). For methyl blue detection, as there are no methyl blue detected in fish sample, the practical applicability for methyl blue detection of the sensing platform of O-FCDs was evaluated by using the standard addition method. In simple terms, methyl blue with different concentrations (2, 4 and 6 μM) were added into the fish sample. The fluorescence intensities were recorded under an excitation wavelength of 490 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of FCDs in living cells

ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of O-FCDs and B-FCDs was evaluated by MTT assay experiment on cholangiocarcinoma cells. In details, cholangiocarcinoma cells were incubated for 48 h until confluency in 96-well plates. Then, different concentrations ranged from 10 μg·mL−1 to 1000 μg·mL−1 of O-FCDs or B-FCDs were added into the 96-wells and cultured for 24 h. Next, MTT (10 μL, 5.0 mg·L-1) was added into each well, and then the cells were continued to be cultured for 4 h. After removing the solutions in wells, the formazan was dissolved by adding dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 150 μL). Finally, the absorbances were recorded at 570 nm after shaking the plate for 10 min.

The cholangiocarcinoma cells were cultured in DMEM medium (containing 10 % of FBS and 2 % of penicillin–streptomycin) in six-well plates for one day. Then, the cells were treated with O-FCDs or B-FCDs with various concentrations (25 μg·mL−1 to 500 μg·mL−1) for various time (0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 6 h). Afterwards, removing the DMEM and washing cells three times with PBS (pH = 7.4). Finally, keeping the cells in PBS for further fluorescence imaging. The fluorescence images were collected by using fluorescent inverted microscope (IX 73, Olympus).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of two FCDs
ce:section.ce:para: 1,3-Dihydroxynaphthalene, a broad reaction inhibitor, is a precursor of FCDs and mainly provides carbon source for FCDs. The two phenolic hydroxyl groups in its structure make it have good antioxidant effect, meanwhile, the highly conjugated structure makes more possibility red shift of FCDs fluorescence color which have more conjugated π-domains [25]. Besides, sulfuric acid facilitates the dehydration and carbonization process who release of a large amount of heat, and doped with a spot of sulfur in the prepared CDs’ structure. Thus, sulfuric acid-ethanol solution was employed as the reaction solvent [26]. The growth process of O-FCDs contained the following steps: In the reaction period of 2 h at 220 °C, several macromolecules and long-chain polymers were formed through oxidative dehydration by the reactions in intermolecular and intramolecular, and then formed the crystal nuclei. Secondly, in the presence of solvent, the carbonization degree of O-FCDs increased and the crystal nucleus gradually grew up. Intermolecular oxidation, dehydration, and carbonization resulted in a large number of chemical groups (such as carbonyl carboxyl and hydroxyl) on the surface of O-FCDs. Sulfuric acid promoted this process [27,28].

Further, “ethanol extraction-water precipitation method” was used in the purification process of FCDs for the first time. As we all know, dialysis, which is separated and purified according to the quantum size, is the most commonly used and effective method for the purification of CDs. However, the maximum pH range of dialysis bag can be used is 2–12, so this method cannot be used for CDs’ dialysis with synthesized by numerous acidic solvents. The ethanol extraction-water precipitation method mainly uses the difference of solubility of FCDs, acid solvent and other unreacted precursor or other products in water and ethanol, and thus this method may be used for the separation of FCDs. In the first step, FCDs would form precipitation in water and can be separated from acidic solvents and other water-soluble products. In the second step, FCDs can be successfully dissolved in ethanol solution. Finally, other small fluorescent molecules which were not cleaned or soluble in ethanol were removed by dialysis (second purification process), and the pure FCDs powder can be obtained by drying. The yields of O-FCDs and B-FCDs were as high as 9.0 % and 21.3 %, and their QYs were calculated as 43.0 % and 13.7 %, respectively. The preparation processes of FCDs were shown in Scheme 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the structural and morphological properties of FCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
From the TEM images in Fig. 1
a-1d, it can be seen that these two kinds of FCDs were uniformly and monodispersed spherical nanodots. The average sizes of O-FCDs and B-FCDs were both at approximately 2.75 nm. The HRTEM images further illustrated that these two types of FCDs exhibited highly crystalline structures. Lattice-fringe spacings of 0.21 nm and 0.34 nm, separately for O-FCDs and B-FCDs, which were belonged to the in-plane lattice spacing of the (100) and (002) facet and of graphite were obtained [29,30]. The lattice spacing of the B-FCDs and O-FCDs decreased with the addition of sulfuric acid, indicating that the introduction of sulfuric acid changed the quantum size of the FCDs, and the size effect can influence the fluorescence wavelength [28]. AFM images (Fig. 1 e−1f) confirmed that these two types of FCDs were nearly spherical and exhibited particle heights of approximately 2.28 nm (B-FCDs) and 3.28 nm (O-FCDs) [31]. Besides, the molecular pictures of the carbonaceous nanostructure of B-FCDs and O-FCDs were further explored by Raman spectral analysis. It can be obviously seen from Fig. 1 g-1h that both of B-FCDs and O-FCDs showed two obvious broad peaks around 1380 and 1600 cm−1, corresponding to the D band and G band, which indicated the presence of sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbon, respectively [32]. Meanwhile, the B-FCDs had higher ID/IG ratio than O-FCDs (B-F CDs: 0.94, B-FCDs: 0.74), revealing the degree of graphitization and carbonization of O-FCDs was higher. The results proved that by the addition of sulfuric acid, the carbonization degree of the precursor increased.

The surface functional group characteristics of two FCDs were demonstrated in Fig. 2
a. The absorption bands at about 3300 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra of B-FCDs and O-FCDs, were ascribing to OH stretching vibration. The peaks at 2950 cm−1, 1598 cm−1, 1208 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of CH, CC/CO, CO/CS bonds [33,34], demonstrating that the surface of both O-FCDs and B-FCDs were full of –OH, CO and other functional groups. In particular, small peaks at 600 cm−1 [17] and 460 cm−1 [35] which separately corresponded to SO4 2-and SS stretching vibrations only existed in the FT-IR spectrum of O-FCDs. These phenomena displayed that sulfur was indeed doped into the structure of O-FCDs. Besides, XPS analysis was used to further confirm the element compositions of two FCDs. As can be seen in Fig. 2 b-c, B-FCDs only showed two typical peaks of C1s (289.4 eV) and O1s (545 eV) in the full spectra. While, O-FCDs showed three peaks of C1s (289.4 eV), O1s (545 eV) and S2p peaks (175.4 eV). These results further proofed the successfully doping of sulfur in the O-FCDs’ structure. Moreover, as described in Fig. 2 d-e, the C1s bands of B-FCDs and O-FCDs showed three significant peaks assigning to CC/CC (284.8 eV), CO/CS (286.2 eV) and CO (287.7 eV) [36]. All the O1s bands of FCDs showed peaks of CO (532 eV) and CO (533 eV) (Shown in Fig. 2 f-g) [37]. The high-solution S2p spectrum of O-FCDs in Fig. 2 h demonstrated four peaks at 169.6, 168.3 169.0 and 167.9 eV, which were attributed to the S2p1/2 and S2p3/2. The existence of these peaks indicated that sulfur appeared mainly in the forms of CS bond and -C-SOx (x = 2, 3, 4, sulfate) groups, respectively [17]. Notably, in contrast to the peaks of B-FCDs (Shown in Table 1 ), the content of C1s (80.86 %) in O-FCDs was not higher than that in B-FCDs (83.59 %). While, compared the content of O1s (16.41 %) in B-FCDs, the content of O1s (18.35 %) in O-FCDs was significantly increased. In detail, the contents of CO and CO both increased (B-FCDs were 2.56 % and 13.85 %, respectively. O-FCDs were 3.68 % and 14.67 %, respectively). The change rules of O1s and C1s (with the redshift of emission wavelength, the C1s of CDs decreased, while the O1s increased) were consistent with the reported literature [38]. These phenomena demonstrated that sulfuric acid promoted the oxidation of precursor in this reaction process.

These all results above showed that sulfur was successfully introduced into O-FCDs, and sulfuric acid promoted the carbonization degree and oxidation degree of CDs in the reaction process. By the addition of sulfuric acid, the O content increased significantly, the lattice spacing decreased, and the fluorescence color of the prepared O-FCDs shifted from blue (B-FCDs) to orange. This showed that the introducing of sulfuric acid was the main factor affecting the color of FCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the optical properties of FCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
By utilizing the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra, the optical properties of FCDs were explored. As displayed in Fig. 3
a, the absorption peaks of O-FCDs, B-FCDs around 250 nm were caused by the π-π* electron transition of CO, CC bond [39]. The absorption peaks of O-FCDs, B-FCDs around 330 nm was due to the electron transition (n-π*) of OH. In addition, the n-π* transition of CS bond situated at about 500 nm existed in O-FCDs’ UV–vis spectra [35], indicating that sulfur had been successfully doped into O-FCDs, and sulfur doping was one of the important reasons for the redshift of O-FCDs fluorescence. Fig. 3 b revealed that the emission peaks of O-FCDs existed at 535 and 575 nm with the excitation wavelength of 490 nm. Moreover, the O-FCDs solution showed light pink color under daylight and emitted orange fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp. Meanwhile, the B-FCDs emitted strong blue fluorescence at the emission peak of 410 nm (excited at 360 nm) (Fig. 3 c). Furthermore, from the Fig. 3 d-3e it is obviously seen that the fluorescence of B-FCDs was excitation-dependent, while the O-FCDs was excitation-independent.

Besides, by using the equation of Egopt = 1240/ kedge, the band-gap energies of FCDs were obtained. kedge represented the wavelength at maximum absorption edge. As exhibited in Fig. 3 f-3h, the band-gap energies decreased from B-FCDs (2.91 eV) to O-FCDs (2.60 eV), elucidating the interband transitions in the FCDs [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of O-FCDs for methyl blue

ce:section.ce:para: It is because the stability of the O-FCDs would affect the effects of the fluorescence detection, so the fluorescence stability of O-FCDs in various pH conditions and in various ionic solutions had been studies firstly. As exhibited in Fig. S1 a, in the pH range of 1–11, the fluorescence of O-FCDs barely changed, revealing that the sensing experiments can be done in this wide pH range. Meanwhile, the influences of different ions were explored through using different ionic solutions (1 mM). It can be clearly known from Fig. S1b-S1c that ions exhibited little impact on the fluorescence of O-FCDs, demonstrating the good stability of O-FCDs in various ionic solutions. Moreover, usually, in the detection process, the fluorescence needed a period of time (commonly 0.5 min–5 min) to stabilize, and thus 0–10 min was used as stabilization time for the detection of target substance. From the Fig. S1d-S1e, it can be known that in 0.5 min, the fluorescence quenching effect of O-FCDs by methyl blue tended to be stable, and thus 0.5 min can be used as the fluorescence detection time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response to methyl blue by O-FCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Different concentrations of 0.3 mL of methyl blue (0–1000 μM) were added into O-FCDs solution (2.7 mL) under the optimal conditions. As displayed in Fig. 4
a-4b, with the increase of the concentrations of methyl blue, two emission peaks at 535 nm and 575 nm of O-FCDs showed obvious downward trends. Fig. 4 c-4d exhibited the response relationship between the methyl blue concentration and S1/S2 (S1: area under the curve from 510 nm to 565 nm, S2 area under the curve from 565 nm to 650 nm) in different concentration ranges. It can be seen that a good linear relationship with S1/S2 value can be found in 0–100 μM. The linear regression equation was S1/S2 = 0.893[C] + 1.9041 (R2 = 0.9949), with the limit of detection (LOD) of 20 nM, where C represented the concentration of methyl blue. Compared with previously reported literature about CDs for methyl blue detection (Only one paper) [40], this sensing method was more sensitive and convenient.

Usually, the actual fish samples may contain methylene blue as well as methyl blue, which is commonly used as a disinfectant in fish ponds. Therefore, the interference of methylene blue, as well as malachite green and hematoxylin (Two other dyes with similar structure) were used to the preliminary evaluation the effects of the O-FCDs fluorescence. The result was shown in Fig.S2 . Except methyl blue, the interference of the other three dyes on the first peak of O-FCDs was relatively small, while there was some interference on the second peak. Therefore, the change of the first peak can be selected to evaluate the interference of other dyes on O-FCDs fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation the quenching mechanism of O-FCDs - methyl blue system
ce:section.ce:para:
To deeply understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism of O-FCDs (Em = 535 nm)-methyl blue and O-FCDs (Em = 535 nm)-methyl blue system, the fluorescence lifetimes and UV–vis analysis were done. As demonstrated in Fig. 5
a-b, the average fluorescence lifetimes of the O-FCDs (Em = 535 nm, 575 nm) were 4.02 ns and 4.23 ns. After adding methyl blue, the corresponding fluorescence lifetimes separately changed to 3.21 ns and 3.88 ns. The nonsignificant changes indicated that IFE and static quenching phenomena may occur between O-FCDs and methyl blue [41]. And that, as displayed in Fig. 5 c, there was some overlap between the absorption peak of methyl blue (480 nm-600 nm) and the excitation wavelength of O-FCDs (400 nm-550 nm), suggesting that the IFE was one of the quenching mechanisms between O-FCDs and methyl blue. Further, to verify the static quenching, the Stern-Volmer equation as followed was applied.
F0/F = 1 + Ksv[c] = 1 + Kqτ0[c].

The τ0 represented the average lifetime of O-FCDs (Em = 535 nm, 575 nm). Kq was the quenching rate constant, Ksv was the quenching constant. Kq and Ksv were gained by conducting the fluorescence detection under different temperatures.

It is clearly seen from Fig. 5 d-e and Table S1 that from 20 °C to 40 °C, Ksv decreased significantly with the increase of temperature. Moreover, the values of Kq of methyl bule at all temperatures were significantly greater than the value of the maximum Kq of collision quenching (1–2 × 1010 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1) [42], confirming the existence of the static quenching between the methyl bule and O-FCDs [43]. Hence, IFE and static quenching may be the quenching mechanisms of O-FCDs - methyl blue system.

In daily life, methyl blue can disinfect fish ponds and prevent fish from getting sick, so it is important to explore the ability of O-FCDs to the detection of methyl blue in fish sample. Adding different concentrations of methyl blue (2, 4 and 6 μM) into the actual sample, and the relevant results were shown in Table 2 . It is obviously seen that the recoveries of methyl blue ranged from 98.52 % to 102.01 % with relative standard deviations (RSDs) less than 5.40 %, revealing that O-FCDs was effective to the detection of methyl blue in fish sample.

Since both FCDs had good fluorescence properties, they can be used for cell imaging. Prior to imaging, the cytotoxicity of both FCDs should be evaluated. The cytotoxicity of FCDs on cholangiocarcinoma cells was studied. It is clearly seen from Fig. 6
a-6b that cell survival rate exceeded 85 % even in the case of 1000 μg·mL−1. Thus, two kinds of FCDs did not have significant cytotoxic effects, so they can be used in cell imaging or other biomedical applications.

Ascribed to the low cytotoxicity and satisfactory fluorescence performance, the potential of two FCDs for cell imaging was be explored. Firstly, the concentration of FCDs for cell imaging was investigated. As exhibited in Fig. 7
a, with the increase of O-FCDs concentration, the imaging effect of the cells became better. The imaging effect was better enough when the concentration of O-FCDs reached 500 μg·mL−1. Thus, the concentration of O-FCDs at 500 μg·mL−1 was used for further cell imaging. For B-FCDs, with the increase of its concentration, although the imaging effect was gradually getting better. while, because of its strong self-fluorescence, poor penetration and low QY, the imaging effect was much worse than that of O-FCDs. Further, the imaging time of two F-CDs were optimized. As the imaging time increased from 30 min to 4 h, the imaging effect of O-FCDs gradually became better. When the imaging time reached 6 h, the fluorescence quenching was caused by the aggregation of O-FCDs, and the imaging effect became worse (Fig. 7 b). Therefore, 4 h can be selected as the imaging time (Fig. 7 c). However, to B-FCDs, with the increase of imaging time, the imaging effect did not significantly improve, which was also due to its own blue fluorescence characteristics. Thus, compared with B-FCDs, O-FCDs had a broader application potential in the field of imaging.

In summary, orange fluorescent O-FCDs with dual-emission wavelength and blue fluorescent B-FCDs with a single-emission wavelength were synthesized by using 1,3-dihydroxynaphthalene as precursor and sulfuric acid as a single variable. Moreover, for the first time, an ethanol extraction-water precipitation method which usually used for the purification of the active ingredients of Traditional Chinese medicine, was used for the purification of FCDs. The results showed that the method could be effectively used for the purification of two FCDs with high yields and high QYs. The results of structural and functional groups analysis of two FCDs were explored and compared. The results showed that the introduction of sulfuric acid would significantly enhance degree of oxidation and graphitization, decrease the lattice spacing, narrow band gap, and thus the fluorescence color of the O-FCDs shifted from blue (B-FCDs) to orange. Interestingly, methyl blue can significantly quench the fluorescence of O-FCDs, so the O-FCDs can be employed for the ratio fluorescence detection of methyl blue with a LOD of 20 nM. In addition, O-FCDs showed great potential for the cell imaging.

Lijuan Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Dandan Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yingyu Gong: Data curation, Investigation. Tian Quan: Data curation, Investigation. Yongqing Tao: Data curation, Investigation. Shaochi Liu: Software, Investigation. Luchun Wang: Software, Investigation. Junji Wang: Software, Investigation. Meng Tian: Software, Investigation. Die Gao: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a joint program of Luzhou Government and Southwest Medical University (2020LZXNYDJ25) and a School-level project of Southwest Medical University (2021ZKMS040).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121963.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
